agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The described architecture has three integration points with distinct failure characteristics. The synchronous Order→Payment→Stripe chain creates a cascading failure risk where Stripe downtime propagates back through Payment Service to Order Service, potentially taking down order processing entirely. The fire-and-forget Notification call silently drops emails with no recovery path. This analysis enumerates 9 failure modes across the integration points and prescribes resilience patterns: circuit breakers, retries with backoff, an outbox pattern for notifications, idempotency keys for payments, and timeout budgets to prevent thread pool exhaustion.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts customer orders, orchestrates payment charging, triggers notifications","boundaries":"Owns order lifecycle state (created, payment_pending, paid, failed). Does NOT own payment or notification logic."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Abstracts payment processing, calls Stripe gateway, returns payment result","boundaries":"Owns payment attempt records and idempotency. Translates Stripe-specific errors into domain payment results."},{"name":"Stripe Gateway (3rd party)","responsibility":"Processes actual card charges","boundaries":"External system outside our control. SLA is Stripe's, not ours."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends order confirmation emails","boundaries":"Owns email templates and delivery tracking. Should be eventually consistent with order state."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP POST /payments with order ID, amount, currency, idempotency key. Returns 200 (charged), 402 (declined), 5xx (infrastructure error).","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral (synchronous, blocking)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Stripe Gateway","contract":"Stripe Charges API (synchronous HTTPS). Payment Service is the anti-corruption layer translating Stripe's model.","coupling_type":"temporal + platform (synchronous, external dependency)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Fire-and-forget HTTP POST /notifications with order details. No response processing.","coupling_type":"fire-and-forget (no delivery guarantee, no temporal coupling on success path)"}],"data_flow":"Customer → Order Service (create order) → Payment Service (charge) → Stripe (process card) → Payment Service (translate result) → Order Service (update order state) → Notification Service (send email). The critical path is Order→Payment→Stripe. Notification is off the critical path but currently has no delivery guarantee.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Prevents cascading failure when Payment Service or Stripe is down. Fails fast instead of exhausting thread pools with hanging requests."},{"pattern":"Retry with Exponential Backoff + Jitter","rationale":"Handles transient failures (network blips, 503s) without thundering herd. Jitter prevents retry storms across concurrent requests."},{"pattern":"Idempotency Key","rationale":"Payment operations MUST be idempotent. Retrying a charge without an idempotency key risks double-charging customers. Stripe natively supports idempotency keys."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Replaces fire-and-forget notification with guaranteed-at-least-once delivery. Order Service writes notification intent to its own DB in the same transaction as the order state change, then a poller/CDC publishes it."},{"pattern":"Timeout Budget","rationale":"Each hop in the sync chain needs explicit timeouts. The Order Service's total timeout must be greater than Payment Service's Stripe timeout, but both must be bounded to prevent thread pool exhaustion."},{"pattern":"Bulkhead","rationale":"Isolate the thread/connection pool used for payment calls from the pool serving other Order Service endpoints, so payment latency spikes don't starve unrelated operations."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Failed notifications after max retries go to a DLQ for manual inspection and replay, ensuring no silent email loss."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Ordering","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, states: created → payment_pending → paid | payment_failed → notified)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["PaymentAttempt (root: PaymentAttemptId, tracks idempotency key, gateway response, retry count)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: NotificationId, tracks delivery status, retry count, last error)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Ordering","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Ordering depends on Payments result but Payments does not know about order lifecycle."},{"upstream":"Stripe Gateway","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Conformist with Anti-Corruption Layer. We conform to Stripe's API but translate through ACL to prevent Stripe model leaking into our domain."},{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via async events. Ordering publishes order-completed events; Notifications consumes them."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous rather than converting to async saga","gains":"Simpler programming model, immediate feedback to customer on payment success/failure, no saga rollback complexity","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is coupled to Payment Service availability. Throughput is bounded by payment latency.","alternatives_considered":["Async saga with compensation (more resilient but significantly more complex for a payment flow where customers expect immediate feedback)","Queued payment processing with polling (adds latency to user experience)"]},{"decision":"Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox","gains":"Guaranteed at-least-once email delivery, no silent failures, auditable notification history","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (outbox table, poller or CDC). Slightly higher write latency on order creation due to outbox insert in same transaction.","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka) directly — but dual-write problem remains without outbox","Keep fire-and-forget with a reconciliation job — simpler but delayed detection of failures"]},{"decision":"Circuit breaker on Payment Service → Stripe","gains":"Fails fast during Stripe outages, preserves Payment Service thread pool, enables graceful degradation","sacrifices":"During open circuit, all payments fail immediately — no queuing of retry. Tuning thresholds requires production traffic analysis.","alternatives_considered":["No circuit breaker, rely on timeouts only — risks thread pool exhaustion under sustained Stripe outage"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"The Anti-Corruption Layer in Payment Service means swapping Stripe for another gateway (Adyen, Braintree) requires changes only inside Payment Service. Order Service is insulated."},{"area":"Notification delivery mechanism","evolution_path":"Moving from outbox-polled HTTP to a message broker (Kafka, SQS) is a transport change behind the outbox abstraction. Notification Service consumer interface stays the same."},{"area":"Circuit breaker configuration","evolution_path":"Thresholds (failure rate, timeout, half-open probe count) should be externalized to config/feature flags for tuning without redeployment."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment coupling","reason":"This is intentionally rigid. Payment is on the critical path — the customer must know immediately if their card was charged. Converting to async would be a major architectural change affecting UX."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once established, the idempotency key format and semantics cannot change without coordinating Order Service, Payment Service, and Stripe. This contract must be versioned carefully."}],"risks":[{"risk":"FM1: Payment Service is down — Order Service blocks, times out, customer gets error","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Circuit breaker on Order→Payment call. When open, return 'payment temporarily unavailable' immediately. Combine with retry (1-2 attempts with short backoff) for transient failures before tripping breaker."},{"risk":"FM2: Stripe is down — Payment Service hangs or errors, cascades to Order Service","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Circuit breaker inside Payment Service on Stripe calls. Strict timeout (e.g., 5s). When breaker opens, Payment Service returns 503 immediately. Order Service's own breaker then handles that."},{"risk":"FM3: Stripe is slow (latency spike, not down) — thread pool exhaustion in Payment Service and Order Service","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Timeout budget: Order Service gives Payment Service 8s total, Payment Service gives Stripe 5s. Bulkhead: isolate payment call thread pool from other Order Service operations. Circuit breaker trips on slow-call percentage threshold, not just errors."},{"risk":"FM4: Double charge — Order Service retries payment call, Payment Service or Stripe processes charge twice","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Idempotency key generated by Order Service, passed through Payment Service to Stripe. Same key = same charge. Payment Service also deduplicates locally by idempotency key before calling Stripe."},{"risk":"FM5: Notification silently lost — fire-and-forget POST fails (network error, Notification Service down), customer never gets email","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace fire-and-forget with transactional outbox pattern. Order Service writes notification intent to outbox table in same DB transaction as order state update. Background poller or CDC publishes to Notification Service. Failed deliveries retry with backoff, eventually go to DLQ."},{"risk":"FM6: Partial failure — payment succeeds at Stripe but response lost on the way back, Order Service thinks payment failed","severity":"high","mitigation":"Idempotency key allows safe retry — Stripe returns the original result. Additionally, implement a payment reconciliation job that polls Stripe for recent charges and reconciles against local payment records."},{"risk":"FM7: Notification Service overwhelmed — burst of orders causes notification backlog, emails delayed significantly","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Rate limiting on outbox poller. Notification Service should have its own backpressure mechanism (reject with 429, poller backs off). Separate queue priority for different notification types."},{"risk":"FM8: Order stuck in payment_pending — Payment Service returns ambiguous error, Order Service doesn't know whether charge happened","severity":"high","mitigation":"Treat ambiguous errors as 'unknown' state. Schedule a deferred check (poll Payment Service / Stripe after delay). Never assume failed without confirmation. Expose stuck-order dashboard for ops."},{"risk":"FM9: Cascading retry storm — multiple Order Service instances simultaneously retry against a recovering Payment Service, overwhelming it","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Jitter on all retry delays. Circuit breaker half-open state allows only a single probe request. Rate limiting on Payment Service ingress."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement resilience in three phases. Phase 1 (immediate): Add idempotency keys to the Order→Payment→Stripe chain and add timeouts to all HTTP calls — these are the highest-severity mitigations with the lowest implementation cost. Phase 2 (short-term): Add circuit breakers (Resilience4j/Polly/Hystrix equivalent) on both sync call sites (Order→Payment, Payment→Stripe) with bulkhead isolation. Phase 3 (medium-term): Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox pattern and add payment reconciliation job.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/PaymentClient — add circuit breaker, retry with backoff+jitter, timeout, idempotency key generation","payment-service/src/clients/StripeClient — add circuit breaker, timeout (5s), retry (1 attempt for 5xx only), pass-through idempotency key","order-service/src/db/migrations/add_outbox_table — create notification_outbox table (id, order_id, payload, status, retry_count, created_at, next_retry_at)","order-service/src/workers/OutboxPoller — poll outbox table, publish to Notification Service, handle failures with backoff","payment-service/src/middleware/IdempotencyFilter — deduplicate incoming payment requests by idempotency key before hitting Stripe","order-service/src/workers/PaymentReconciler — periodic job that reconciles payment_pending orders against Stripe/Payment Service"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use a resilience library (Resilience4j for JVM, Polly for .NET, cockatiel for Node.js) rather than hand-rolling circuit breakers. Configure circuit breakers via external config. Idempotency keys should be UUIDv4 generated by Order Service and stored with the order. Outbox poller should use SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for distributed-safe polling.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT retry payment calls without idempotency keys — double-charge risk","Do NOT set timeouts longer than the upstream caller's timeout — causes the upstream to timeout first, wasting work","Do NOT use fire-and-forget for any operation where delivery matters","Do NOT share thread pools between payment calls and other endpoints — one slow dependency can starve everything","Do NOT treat all Stripe errors as retryable — 4xx (card declined, invalid request) must NOT be retried","Do NOT implement circuit breaker without monitoring/alerting on state transitions — you need to know when the breaker opens"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-known distributed systems failure modes","Idempotency key requirement — this is non-negotiable for payment flows","Outbox pattern for notifications — industry-standard replacement for fire-and-forget","Circuit breaker and timeout placement — standard resilience patterns"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific timeout values (5s, 8s) — these need tuning based on actual Stripe latency percentiles in production","Circuit breaker thresholds — failure rate %, slow-call duration, ring buffer size all need production data","Whether bulkhead should be thread-pool-based or semaphore-based — depends on the HTTP client being used"],"assumptions":["Order Service and Payment Service are independently deployable services with separate databases","Stripe is the only payment gateway (no multi-gateway routing)","Email is the only notification channel (no SMS, push, etc.)","The system uses a relational database that supports transactions (for outbox pattern)","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a polling-based outbox is sufficient (vs CDC for high throughput)"]}}

Let me think through the failure modes systematically:

1. Order→Payment sync call failures
2. Payment→Stripe sync call failures
3. Order→Notification fire-and-forget failures
4. Cascading failures

Ordering publishes order-completed events; Notifications consumes them."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous rather than converting to async saga","gains":"Simpler programming model, immediate feedback to customer on payment success/failure, no saga rollback complexity","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is coupled to Payment Service availability. Throughput is bounded by payment latency.","alternatives_considered":["Async saga with compensation (more resilient but significantly more complex for a payment flow where customers expect immediate feedback)","Queued payment processing with polling (adds latency to user experience)"]},{"decision":"Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox","gains":"Guaranteed at-least-once email delivery, no silent failures, auditable notification history","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (outbox table, poller or CDC). Slightly higher write latency on order creation due to outbox insert in same transaction.","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka) directly — but dual-write problem remains without outbox","Keep fire-and-forget with a reconciliation job — simpler but delayed detection of failures"]},{"decision":"Circuit breaker on Payment Service → Stripe","gains":"Fails fast during Stripe outages, preserves Payment Service thread pool, enables graceful degradation","sacrifices":"During open circuit, all payments fail immediately — no queuing of retry. Tuning thresholds requires production traffic analysis.","alternatives_considered":["No circuit breaker, rely on timeouts only — risks thread pool exhaustion under sustained Stripe outage"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"The Anti-Corruption Layer in Payment Service means swapping Stripe for another gateway (Adyen, Braintree) requires changes only inside Payment Service. Order Service is insulated."},{"area":"Notification delivery mechanism","evolution_path":"Moving from outbox-polled HTTP to a message broker (Kafka, SQS) is a transport change behind the outbox abstraction. Notification Service consumer interface stays the same."},{"area":"Circuit breaker configuration","evolution_path":"Thresholds (failure rate, timeout, half-open probe count) should be externalized to config/feature flags for tuning without redeployment."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment coupling","reason":"This is intentionally rigid. Payment is on the critical path — the customer must know immediately if their card was charged. Converting to async would be a major architectural change affecting UX."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once established, the idempotency key format and semantics cannot change without coordinating Order Service, Payment Service, and Stripe. This contract must be versioned carefully."}],"risks":[{"risk":"FM1: Payment Service is down — Order Service blocks, times out, customer gets error","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Circuit breaker on Order→Payment call. When open, return 'payment temporarily unavailable' immediately. Combine with retry (1-2 attempts with short backoff) for transient failures before tripping breaker."},{"risk":"FM2: Stripe is down — Payment Service hangs or errors, cascades to Order Service","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Circuit breaker inside Payment Service on Stripe calls. Strict timeout (e.g., 5s). When breaker opens, Payment Service returns 503 immediately. Order Service's own breaker then handles that."},{"risk":"FM3: Stripe is slow (latency spike, not down) — thread pool exhaustion in Payment Service and Order Service","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Timeout budget: Order Service gives Payment Service 8s total, Payment Service gives Stripe 5s. Bulkhead: isolate payment call thread pool from other Order Service operations. Circuit breaker trips on slow-call percentage threshold, not just errors."},{"risk":"FM4: Double charge — Order Service retries payment call, Payment Service or Stripe processes charge twice","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Idempotency key generated by Order Service, passed through Payment Service to Stripe. Same key = same charge. Payment Service also deduplicates locally by idempotency key before calling Stripe."},{"risk":"FM5: Notification silently lost — fire-and-forget POST fails (network error, Notification Service down), customer never gets email","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace fire-and-forget with transactional outbox pattern. Order Service writes notification intent to outbox table in same DB transaction as order state update. Background poller or CDC publishes to Notification Service. Failed deliveries retry with backoff, eventually go to DLQ."},{"risk":"FM6: Partial failure — payment succeeds at Stripe but response lost on the way back, Order Service thinks payment failed","severity":"high","mitigation":"Idempotency key allows safe retry — Stripe returns the original result. Additionally, implement a payment reconciliation job that polls Stripe for recent charges and reconciles against local payment records."},{"risk":"FM7: Notification Service overwhelmed — burst of orders causes notification backlog, emails delayed significantly","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Rate limiting on outbox poller. Notification Service should have its own backpressure mechanism (reject with 429, poller backs off). Separate queue priority for different notification types."},{"risk":"FM8: Order stuck in payment_pending — Payment Service returns ambiguous error, Order Service doesn't know whether charge happened","severity":"high","mitigation":"Treat ambiguous errors as 'unknown' state. Schedule a deferred check (poll Payment Service / Stripe after delay). Never assume failed without confirmation. Expose stuck-order dashboard for ops."},{"risk":"FM9: Cascading retry storm — multiple Order Service instances simultaneously retry against a recovering Payment Service, overwhelming it","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Jitter on all retry delays. Circuit breaker half-open state allows only a single probe request. Rate limiting on Payment Service ingress."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement resilience in three phases. Phase 1 (immediate): Add idempotency keys to the Order→Payment→Stripe chain and add timeouts to all HTTP calls — these are the highest-severity mitigations with the lowest implementation cost. Phase 2 (short-term): Add circuit breakers (Resilience4j/Polly/Hystrix equivalent) on both sync call sites (Order→Payment, Payment→Stripe) with bulkhead isolation. Phase 3 (medium-term): Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox pattern and add payment reconciliation job.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/PaymentClient — add circuit breaker, retry with backoff+jitter, timeout, idempotency key generation","payment-service/src/clients/StripeClient — add circuit breaker, timeout (5s), retry (1 attempt for 5xx only), pass-through idempotency key","order-service/src/db/migrations/add_outbox_table — create notification_outbox table (id, order_id, payload, status, retry_count, created_at, next_retry_at)","order-service/src/workers/OutboxPoller — poll outbox table, publish to Notification Service, handle failures with backoff","payment-service/src/middleware/IdempotencyFilter — deduplicate incoming payment requests by idempotency key before hitting Stripe","order-service/src/workers/PaymentReconciler — periodic job that reconciles payment_pending orders against Stripe/Payment Service"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use a resilience library (Resilience4j for JVM, Polly for .NET, cockatiel for Node.js) rather than hand-rolling circuit breakers. Configure circuit breakers via external config. Idempotency keys should be UUIDv4 generated by Order Service and stored with the order. Outbox poller should use SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for distributed-safe polling.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT retry payment calls without idempotency keys — double-charge risk","Do NOT set timeouts longer than the upstream caller's timeout — causes the upstream to timeout first, wasting work","Do NOT use fire-and-forget for any operation where delivery matters","Do NOT share thread pools between payment calls and other endpoints — one slow dependency can starve everything","Do NOT treat all Stripe errors as retryable — 4xx (card declined, invalid request) must NOT be retried","Do NOT implement circuit breaker without monitoring/alerting on state transitions — you need to know when the breaker opens"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-known distributed systems failure modes","Idempotency key requirement — this is non-negotiable for payment flows","Outbox pattern for notifications — industry-standard replacement for fire-and-forget","Circuit breaker and timeout placement — standard resilience patterns"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific timeout values (5s, 8s) — these need tuning based on actual Stripe latency percentiles in production","Circuit breaker thresholds — failure rate %, slow-call duration, ring buffer size all need production data","Whether bulkhead should be thread-pool-based or semaphore-based — depends on the HTTP client being used"],"assumptions":["Order Service and Payment Service are independently deployable services with separate databases","Stripe is the only payment gateway (no multi-gateway routing)","Email is the only notification channel (no SMS, push, etc.)","The system uses a relational database that supports transactions (for outbox pattern)","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a polling-based outbox is sufficient (vs CDC for high throughput)"]}}
